title: Orbital-Angular-Momentum Entangled Photon Emission from Circular Currents in Semiconductor-Superconductor Structures
authors: Avi Koriat, Ankit Kumar, Alex Hayat
abstract: We theoretically demonstrate that a superconducting circular current induced in a semiconductor results in emission of orbital-angular-momentum (OAM) entangled photon pairs upon carrier recombination. Combining the macroscopic Ginzburg-Landau theory and the microscopic Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) theory, we investigate the emission of a superconducting light-emitting diode (SLED) with a spatially varying phase profile in the superconducting order parameter. We show that in the active region of the SLED with a circular supercurrent, radiative recombination processes inherit the order parameter phase and result in photon pairs emitted into modes of different OAM quantum numbers. We demonstrate that coherent superposition of superconducting qubit eigenstates can also be mapped onto a coherent superposition of emitted photon states. We also show that other recombination processes due to thermally excited quasi particles do not significantly degrade the state purity. Our results introduce an original scheme for generating OAM-entangled photons enabling a new method of transmitting superconducting qubit information to photonic channels thereby bridging the gap between solid-state and photon-based platforms for quantum communications and information processing.
summary: The paper theoretically demonstrates a method to generate OAM-entangled photon pairs from a superconducting LED by utilizing the spatial phase of a circular supercurrent. This mechanism allows for the direct mapping of superconducting qubit states onto photonic OAM modes, providing a potential interface for hybrid quantum networks.
main_problem: The need for a quantum interface to transfer quantum information from superconducting qubits (limited by local environment) to photonic channels (ideal for long-distance communication) using high-dimensional OAM encoding.
main_result: Demonstrated that a superconducting circular current can imprint its spatial phase onto emitted photon pairs, creating OAM-entangled photons that faithfully map the qubit's superposition state.
method: Theoretical modeling using a combination of Ginzburg-Landau theory for the superconducting phase and BCS theory for the semiconductor recombination, utilizing Bogoliubov transformations and perturbation theory.
model_or_system: A superconductor-semiconductor-superconductor (S-N-P-S) structure acting as a Superconducting LED (SLED), featuring a ring-shaped superconducting cathode and a disk-shaped anode with a p-n quantum well.
key_observables: Two-photon emission rate, OAM quantum numbers, photon-energy detuning, state purity, and Bell-state fidelity.
important_parameters: Winding number (ls), voltage bias (V), temperature (T/Tc), and the enhancement factor (L/Lphi) representing the ratio of device circumference to coherence length.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a well-defined superconducting gap and relies on the ability to detect specific OAM modes; fidelity is sensitive to thermal excitations and gap narrowing.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device architecture, side view with optical cavity, and band diagrams. Fig 2 displays the two-photon emission rate vs. detuning and temperature for CP and BQP processes. Fig 5 shows Bell-state fidelity as a function of temperature for different enhancement factors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of platform bridging, describes the SLED device architecture and theoretical framework (GL and BCS), details the mathematical derivation of photon emission and OAM conservation, evaluates the impact of thermal quasiparticles on fidelity, and concludes with the implications for quantum communication.
why_interesting: It proposes a novel mechanism for high-dimensional quantum state transfer between solid-state qubits and photonic modes, bridging the gap between superconducting circuits and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Spintronics.note: The paper directly addresses the optics-spintronics interface by describing how a superconducting current (spin-related transport) drives coherent, mode-selective photon emission.
